dialogue.utterance: Dispatch to Unit 300 for breathing problems at a residence in Richmond.
Hello, I'm Jerry, an EMT with the rescue squad. What brought you to call 911 today, sir?
I've had trouble breathing for a few days, and it has progressively gotten worse.
Can you tell me your name, sir?
What county are we in right now, sir?
Can you tell me the date today and how you got to this situation, sir?
You look alert and oriented. Are you aware of what's happening, sir?
Yes, I do. I've had COPD before, and I think it's flaring up again.
We need to assess your airway. May we do that now, sir?
Sure. I'm not having any immediate breathing trouble, just shortness of breath.
I'd like to look at your chest and listen to your breathing. Your OK with that?
Respiratory rate is 26, and you are using accessory muscles to breathe. May I check your oxygen saturation, please?
Your oxygen saturation is 98 percent while breathing room air.
I'm going to listen to your lungs now, sir. If you can take deep breaths.
Sounds from the lower lung fields are diminished, with expiratory wheezing in the upper lobes. Your chest is symmetric and atraumatic.
I need to check your pulse and blood pressure now. Your okay with that?
Your pulse is 122, in rhythm. Your skin is pink, warm, dry, with normal turgor.
Blood pressure reads 145 over 80. No signs of shock or circulation issues so far.
May we get some more background about your condition to help with care today?
When did the breathing troubles start, and have you tried any treatments since it began?
It began about 3 or 4 days ago. I've been using my inhaler but it's not helping much anymore.
What medical conditions do you have, such as COPD or heart history?
I have COPD, had a past heart attack, and deal with high blood pressure.
What medications and inhalers are you currently using to manage symptoms?
I use Albuterol and Atrovent inhalers. I take Lantus, metformin, and Diltiazem for diabetes and blood pressure.
When was your last meal or drink before the breathing started worsening?
I can't recall the exact time, but it was sometime in the morning.
I need to look at your skin to check for rashes or texture changes. May I?
Skin is dry but normal in turgor and warm with no visible rashes.
I'd like to look at your head and neck next. That okay with you, sir?
Head is normocephalic. No tracheal deviation or JVD noted.
I'll assess your neurological status quickly. Can you answer some questions, sir?
You are alert and oriented to person, place, time and event.
What position are you most comfortable in to help with breathing?
I sit up to help. I can walk if I need to but prefer to stay seated.
I'm applying a 4-lead ECG now. Just stay still while it's set up.
EKG shows sinus tachycardia. Heart is in a fast but regular rhythm.
I need to give you a nebulizer with Albuterol and Atrovent to open your airway. You okay with that?
Nebulizer is working. It contains 5 mg Albuterol and 0.5 mg Atrovent with 8 LPM oxygen. This treatment is called a DuoNeb.
We're also setting up an IV. It will give you Dexamethasone for inflammation in your lungs.
The nebulizer is done. We're moving on to IV Dexamethasone now.
Your pulse has dropped to 107 and is still in rhythm. You're showing signs of improvement.
Breathing rate is now 31 with labored effort. Wheezing is still reactive in breath sounds.
Oxygen saturation remains steady at 99 percent following treatment.
Initial glucose was 84 mg/dL. No further monitoring occurred during transport.
EKG continues to show sinus tachycardia with no new abnormalities present.
Can I assess your head, eyes, nose, and throat for any signs of irritation or injury?
Head, eyes, nose, and throat all appear normal with no redness, swelling, or drainage noted.
Your airway is patent and no foreign bodies or obstructions are visible at this time.
Are you experiencing any kind of pain right now besides the breathing issue?
No, I am not in pain. The breathing is my only issue.
Based on EKG and vital signs, no cardiac cause. Treatment follows COPD respiratory distress protocol.
How do you feel now after the nebulizer and IV was given?
The nebulizer helped somewhat. I would say it's about a 2 or 3 out of 10 improvement.
Blood pressure is now 119 over 70 from earlier. Still trending in range.
We're heading to Retreat Hospital as requested. I'll help you secure the stretcher.
The stretcher is secured in a comfortable position for you. Let's move with care now.
I'll assist you in ambulating to the stretcher, if that's what you prefer.
That's okay, I prefer to walk by myself with minimal help.
We've placed an ETCO2 nasal cannula to monitor your breathing during transport.
You are now securely fastened on the stretcher. We are ready to move.
Just checking again if you are experiencing any pain at this time.
No, I still feel no pain at all. Breathing is still the main issue.
We'll be arriving at Retreat Hospital in ED Room 4, as confirmed via radio earlier.
Patient will arrive in ED Room 4 of Retreat Hospital. Continuous monitoring is in place.
EMTs Jerry and Rebecca will hand over to ED staff for continued care. Trending and monitoring will be shared upon arrival.
The attending RN will assume care of the patient for further treatment and evaluation.
During transport, your condition remained stable with no new symptoms or deterioration.
Your pulse is now 107, still in a regular rhythm.
Respiratory rate is now 31 and still labored, with reactive wheezing present.
Your oxygen saturation remains stable at 99 percent.
EKG reads the same as before-sinus tachycardia with no new changes.
We are approaching the hospital. Our summary for the RN is ready upon arrival.
I will help you transfer from the stretcher to the hospital bed.
I can ambulate to the bed with minor assistance if needed.
No pain reported throughout the call. Vital signs and EKG remained stable with no new changes.
I have handed over all pertinent information to the RN. You are in expert care now.
All events occurred without incident. Thank you for trusting our care.
The patient ambulated from the stretcher to the hospital bed with assistance.
No changes in condition during transport. All findings and treatments are included in the report.
Glucose level remained at 84 mg/dL and did not need additional monitoring.
Patient is safely delivered to the hospital. Report has been transferred.
All patient information has been shared with the RN for continued care.
Patient ambulated from the curb to the work, then onto the stretcher and into the hospital.
EMS treatment protocol for COPD was followed. No significant cardiac findings.
Transport was initiated per patient request with the individual's consent throughout.
No further interventions required at this time. EMT care is complete.
No worsening or new symptoms were reported by the patient during transport.
Final vitals were stable, with no changes to pulse, blood pressure, or oxygen saturation.
Albuterol, Atrovent, and Dexamethasone were all administered with positive response.
Patient transported to Retreat Doctors' Hospital as requested per call narrative.
No significant bleeding, trauma, or worsening of symptoms was noted.
No incident reports were needed. Patient was alert and cooperative throughout.
The ambulance will now be transported to a reset station for preparation for next call.
EPC and ETA were recorded. No calls on hold or deferrals occurred during the call.
Final check for pain was performed. None reported at any point during care.
Patient was accepted for further evaluation and care at the hospital. No complaints at handover.
Receiving RN gave a satisfactory report and accepted patient care. Transport official complete.
All documentation is signed and ready for dispatch review.
EMTs Jerry and Rebecca are returning to base per normal operational procedure.
